easyHint: A rare type of spin that mirrors the orthodox, but comes from the other hand.
mediumHint: Kuldeep Yadav brought this back into the spotlight for India.
hardHint: Named after Ellis Achong, a player of Chinese descent who played for West Indies in the 1930s.

easyHint: The mysterious third variation of an off-spinner.
mediumHint: Saqlain Mushtaq invented the second, but who really perfected this third one is still debated.
hardHint: Saeed Ajmal claimed he could bowl this, but the ICC biomechanics test had other ideas.

easyHint: A leg-spinner's delivery that skids low and fast instead of turning.
mediumHint: Shane Warne claimed to have invented it to trick English batsmen in the 90s.
hardHint: Often confused with the flipper, some say Warne entirely fabricated its existence just to mess with media and opponents.

easyHint: Getting out without facing a single legal delivery.
mediumHint: Usually involves a tragic mix-up and a run-out at the non-striker's end on the first ball.
hardHint: Harshal Patel suffered this fate against SRH when he was run out without facing a ball.

easyHint: Getting out on the first ball in both innings of a Test match.
mediumHint: Ajit Agarkar famously avoided this against Australia after his legendary string of zeroes.
hardHint: Virender Sehwag suffered this ignominy against England at Edgbaston in 2011.

easyHint: Going on one knee and flicking the ball directly over the wicketkeeper's head.
mediumHint: Named after a Sri Lankan opener who devastated bowlers in the 2009 T20 World Cup.
hardHint: Originally inspired by the 'Marillier shot', but adapted to fast pacers instead of just spinners.

easyHint: A graceful pull shot played while balancing on one leg.
mediumHint: Kapil Dev made it iconic, and Ranveer Singh practiced it for months for a movie.
hardHint: The silhouette of this shot resembles the cosmic dance of Lord Shiva.

easyHint: Running out the non-striker before bowling the ball.
mediumHint: Ashwin made this a global debate in the IPL when he dismissed Jos Buttler.
hardHint: Named after an Indian legend who first did it to Bill Brown in 1947 in Australia.

easyHint: When a batsman willfully blocks the fielding side from making a play.
mediumHint: Inzamam-ul-Haq was given out this way against India in 2006 for stopping a throw with his bat.
hardHint: Len Hutton was the first to be dismissed this way in Test cricket in 1951.

easyHint: Failing to arrive at the crease within the allowed minutes.
mediumHint: Angelo Mathews was the first victim of this in international cricket.
hardHint: Sourav Ganguly almost suffered this against South Africa in 2007 because he couldn't find his bat.

easyHint: A batsman gets out by using their hands to stop the ball from hitting the stumps.
mediumHint: Mohinder Amarnath and Steve Waugh have both fallen prey to this rare dismissal.
hardHint: In 2017, the ICC absorbed this law entirely into 'Obstructing the Field'.

easyHint: Making contact with the ball twice with the bat intentionally.
mediumHint: You can only do it legally to protect your wicket, not to score runs.
hardHint: No player has ever been dismissed this way in an International match, but it exists in the rulebooks.

easyHint: The large board placed behind the bowler to help the batsman see the ball.
mediumHint: It is white for red-ball cricket and black for white-ball cricket.
hardHint: Sachin Tendulkar once famously stopped a bowler because a fan was moving near this object.

easyHint: A lower-order batsman sent in at the end of the day to survive.
mediumHint: Jason Gillespie famously scored a double century while playing this role.
hardHint: Syed Kirmani often played this role for India, shielding the fragile middle order of the 70s.

easyHint: A fielding position right next to the slips.
mediumHint: It's the spot where you catch the thick outside edges that fly backward of square.
hardHint: In gully cricket, this is where the uncle stands who refuses to give your ball back.

easyHint: A fielding position dangerously close to the batsman on the off-side.
mediumHint: You usually wear a helmet and shin guards when standing here for a spinner.
hardHint: Eknath Solkar was arguably the greatest Indian fielder in this perilous position during the spin quartet era.

easyHint: A spin delivery flicked out from between the thumb and bent middle finger.
mediumHint: R Ashwin brought this into the limelight against Mendis in the late 2000s.
hardHint: It was originally called the 'Sodukku ball' in the streets of Chennai before international fame.

easyHint: A wristy whip that ends with the bat twirling over the head.
mediumHint: MS Dhoni used it to dig out deadly yorkers and send them into the stands.
hardHint: It was originally taught to Dhoni by his childhood friend Santosh Lal, who called it the 'Thappad Shot'.

easyHint: Uses sound waves to check if the ball touched the bat.
mediumHint: Often shows a spike on the graph when the ball passes the edge.
hardHint: Invented by Allan Plaskett, it was once fooled by the sound of a bat hitting a pad or the ground.

easyHint: Uses infrared cameras to detect friction on the bat or pad.
mediumHint: Shows a bright white mark where the ball made contact.
hardHint: Banned temporarily because silicone tape on the edges of bats was rumored to prevent it from showing marks.

easyHint: The ball-tracking system used for LBW decisions.
mediumHint: Shows the pitch, impact, and wickets hitting on the big screen.
hardHint: Its inventor, Paul Hawkins, played minor counties cricket in England and based it on missile tracking tech.

easyHint: The mathematical method used to calculate targets in rain-affected matches.
mediumHint: Now known as DLS after Steven Stern updated the formula.
hardHint: South Africa once needed 22 runs off 1 ball in a 1992 World Cup semi-final due to a predecessor of this system.

easyHint: When an old ball swings towards the shiny side instead of away from it.
mediumHint: Pioneered by the Pakistan fast bowlers like Imran Khan, Wasim Akram, and Waqar Younis.
hardHint: Requires the ball to be scuffed up on one side, which once led to the infamous 'Bottle top' controversy in 2006.

easyHint: A batsman promoted up the order specifically to score quick runs.
mediumHint: Sanath Jayasuriya and Romesh Kaluwitharana redefined this role in the 1996 World Cup.
hardHint: Sunil Narine was successfully deployed in this exact role by KKR, completely altering IPL powerplays.

easyHint: The ultimate tie-breaker after a T20 match ends level.
mediumHint: Six balls per side, two wickets maximum.
hardHint: The 2019 World Cup final was decided not by runs in this phase, but by a boundary countback rule.

easyHint: A cheeky chip down the middle of the goal during a penalty.
mediumHint: Zidane did it in a World Cup final, kissing the crossbar.
hardHint: Named after Antonin, who first used it to win the 1976 European Championship against West Germany.

easyHint: Kicking the ball by wrapping one leg around the back of the other.
mediumHint: Angel Di Maria often uses this to cross the ball because he hates his weak foot.
hardHint: Pele claims to have performed it in the 1950s, but it was popularized by Ricardo Infante in Argentina.

easyHint: A dribbling move where you push the ball outside, then quickly drag it inside with the same foot.
mediumHint: Ronaldinho popularized this 'flip-flap' skill globally.
hardHint: Invented by Japanese-Brazilian player Sérgio Echigo, who taught it to Rivellino.

easyHint: Striking or passing the ball with the outside of the boot to apply curve.
mediumHint: Ricardo Quaresma built his entire career highlight reel around this technique.
hardHint: Luka Modric used this perfectly to assist Rodrygo against Chelsea in the Champions League.

easyHint: Faking a pass or cross, then dragging the ball behind your standing leg to change direction.
mediumHint: Invented by a legendary Dutch player who revolutionized the sport.
hardHint: First unleashed on the world stage against Swedish defender Jan Olsson in the 1974 World Cup.

easyHint: Kicking the ball through the opponent's legs.
mediumHint: Lionel Messi does this so often it feels like he's insulting defenders.
hardHint: In India, playing on the streets, this humiliating move is often loudly called a 'Galla'.

easyHint: Throwing your body in the air and kicking the ball backward over your head.
mediumHint: Wayne Rooney did a famous one against Man City, and Ronaldo against Juventus.
hardHint: Also known as the 'Chalaca', originating in Peru, though Leonidas da Silva made it famous in Brazil.

easyHint: Diving forward and kicking the ball away with your heels above your back.
mediumHint: Olivier Giroud won a Puskas award for scoring with this acrobatic move.
hardHint: Rene Higuita shocked the world by doing this to save a shot at Wembley in 1995.

easyHint: A style of play focused on short passing and maintaining possession.
mediumHint: Pep Guardiola's Barcelona team became legendary for perfecting this system.
hardHint: The term was supposedly coined by Spanish broadcaster Andrés Montes, mimicking the ticking of a clock.

easyHint: Instantly attempting to win the ball back immediately after losing possession.
mediumHint: Jurgen Klopp brought this intense style to Borussia Dortmund and Liverpool.
hardHint: Ralf Rangnick is often credited as the 'godfather' of this heavy metal football philosophy in Germany.

easyHint: An ultra-defensive system prioritizing a solid backline and man-marking.
mediumHint: Helenio Herrera used this to make Inter Milan a dominant force in the 1960s.
hardHint: Italian for 'door-bolt', it famously utilized a 'sweeper' or 'libero' behind the main defenders.

easyHint: A tactical theory where any outfield player can take over the role of any other player.
mediumHint: The 1970s Ajax and Netherlands national team stunned the world with this fluid setup.
hardHint: Pioneered by Rinus Michels, heavily reliant on spatial awareness and extreme stamina.

easyHint: A center forward who frequently drops deep into midfield to disrupt the defense.
mediumHint: Messi thrived in this role under Guardiola, completely destroying Real Madrid 6-2.
hardHint: Francesco Totti famously popularized this specific role at Roma under Luciano Spalletti.

easyHint: A goalkeeper who acts almost like an extra defender outside the penalty box.
mediumHint: Manuel Neuer redefined this role at the 2014 World Cup for Germany.
hardHint: Lev Yashin was one of the earliest to play this way, venturing far out of his box in the 1960s.

easyHint: A deep-lying playmaker who dictates the tempo of the game from in front of the defense.
mediumHint: Andrea Pirlo is universally considered the absolute master of this elegant position.
hardHint: Italian for 'director', requiring immense vision but not necessarily high physical stamina.

easyHint: An attacking playmaker who operates between the midfield and the forwards.
mediumHint: Diego Maradona, Zinedine Zidane, and Kaka excelled in this classic 'number 10' space.
hardHint: In Italian, it literally translates to 'three-quarters', indicating where they operate on the pitch.

easyHint: A central midfielder who drifts wide to operate in the half-spaces.
mediumHint: Kevin De Bruyne often plays this role, crossing dangerously from wide areas.
hardHint: Italian for 'half-winger', originally used in the WM formation to describe inside forwards.

easyHint: A tireless midfielder who defends in their own penalty area and scores in the opponent's.
mediumHint: Yaya Toure, Steven Gerrard, and Arturo Vidal were iconic in this energetic role.
hardHint: Requires elite stamina and 'lung capacity', often referred to as an 'engine room' player.

easyHint: Rolling the ball up one leg and kicking it over your opponent's head.
mediumHint: Neymar loves doing this, much to the anger of defenders who usually foul him right after.
hardHint: Jay-Jay Okocha famously executed this against Ray Parlour in the Premier League.

easyHint: Chipping the ball directly over a defender's head and catching it on the other side.
mediumHint: Pele famously did this to a defender inside the penalty box in the 1958 World Cup final.
hardHint: Named after a Mexican hat because the ball forms an arc that resembles one.

easyHint: Moving the foot completely over the ball without touching it to confuse the defender.
mediumHint: Cristiano Ronaldo used to do ten of these in a row during his early Manchester United days.
hardHint: The original master of this was 'Il Fenomeno' Ronaldo, using it to completely freeze goalkeepers.

easyHint: Striking the ball while it's in the air, before it touches the ground.
mediumHint: Zidane's Champions League final winner against Bayer Leverkusen is the most famous one.
hardHint: Marco van Basten's impossible angle goal in Euro 88 is the gold standard for this technique.

easyHint: Striking the ball exactly at the moment it bounces on the ground.
mediumHint: Produces incredible power and is notoriously difficult to keep below the crossbar.
hardHint: Benjamin Pavard won the 2018 World Cup Goal of the Tournament using this exact striking technique.

easyHint: Throwing yourself parallel to the ground to strike the ball with your forehead.
mediumHint: Robin van Persie scored a legendary one against Spain in the 2014 World Cup.
hardHint: Often called 'The Flying Dutchman' goal, it requires extreme bravery and timing.

easyHint: The video system used by referees to review decisions.
mediumHint: It involves drawing literal lines on a screen to check for offsides, enraging fans.
hardHint: Introduced in the 2018 World Cup, its 'clear and obvious error' protocol is still debated weekly.

easyHint: Jumping over the bar backwards instead of forwards.
mediumHint: Revolutionized the high jump at the 1968 Olympics.
hardHint: Dick Fosbury invented it because he was terrible at the traditional 'straddle' technique.

easyHint: The large main pack of riders in a cycling race.
mediumHint: Riding in this group saves up to 40% of energy due to drafting.
hardHint: If there is a crash in this group, it's called a 'pile-up', often occurring in the Tour de France.

easyHint: A special pawn capture in chess.
mediumHint: Can only be done immediately after a pawn moves two squares forward.
hardHint: Often causes beginners to accuse their opponent of cheating because they don't know the rule.

easyHint: Scoring three under par on a single hole in golf.
mediumHint: Much rarer than a Hole in One, usually achieved on a Par 5.
hardHint: Also known in the US as a 'Double Eagle'.

easyHint: A 40-all tie in tennis.
mediumHint: You must win two consecutive points from this state to win the game.
hardHint: The longest game involving this went to 37 of them in a single game in 1975.

easyHint: A forward-facing jump with three and a half rotations in the air.
mediumHint: Tonya Harding and Midori Ito were famous for landing this difficult figure skating move.
hardHint: The only standard jump in figure skating that takes off from a forward edge.

easyHint: A left-handed stance in boxing.
mediumHint: Manny Pacquiao and Oleksandr Usyk are famous for fighting from this stance.
hardHint: The term originally comes from baseball, referring to left-handed pitchers facing south in early stadiums.

easyHint: Trapping the king so it cannot escape.
mediumHint: The ultimate goal in chess that immediately ends the game.
hardHint: Derived from the Persian phrase 'Shah Mat', meaning the King is helpless.

easyHint: A gymnastics apparatus that requires circular leg swings without stopping.
mediumHint: Only men compete on this specific apparatus in the Olympics.
hardHint: Max Whitlock won double Olympic gold on this extremely technically demanding apparatus.

easyHint: Scoring one under par on a golf hole.
mediumHint: The most common positive scoring term you hear at the Masters.
hardHint: The term was coined in 1899 by Ab Smith in New Jersey, using a slang term meaning 'excellent'.

easyHint: Winning all four major tournaments in a single year.
mediumHint: Serena Williams almost did this in 2015 but lost in the US Open.
hardHint: Steffi Graf is the only player to achieve the 'Golden' version of this, including an Olympic Gold.

easyHint: Winning a fight by rendering the opponent unable to stand up before the count of 10.
mediumHint: Mike Tyson was famous for securing these in the very first round.
hardHint: A 'Technical' version of this occurs when the referee stops the fight because a boxer cannot defend themselves.

easyHint: Forcing an opponent to tap out in combat sports.
mediumHint: Khabib Nurmagomedov used a neck crank to achieve this against Conor McGregor.
hardHint: The 'Gogoplata' is a rare and extremely complex variation involving the shin across the throat.

easyHint: Scoring 6 points by carrying the ball into the endzone.
mediumHint: Tom Brady has thrown more of these than anyone else in NFL history.
hardHint: A 'Pick-Six' is a dramatic version of this, scored by the defense immediately after an interception.

easyHint: Hitting the ball out of the park and rounding all the bases.
mediumHint: Babe Ruth and Barry Bonds are historically famous for these.
hardHint: An 'Inside-the-park' version is extremely rare and usually involves defensive blunders.

easyHint: Jumping high and forcefully pushing the ball through the hoop.
mediumHint: Michael Jordan made this an art form from the free-throw line.
hardHint: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar rarely did this, preferring his signature 'Skyhook' instead.

easyHint: Catching a pass in mid-air and dunking it before landing.
mediumHint: LeBron James and Dwyane Wade produced an iconic photo doing this.
hardHint: The term actually originated from a French circus acrobat phrase meaning 'let's go'.

easyHint: Failing to serve the ball into the correct box.
mediumHint: Doing this twice in a row gives the opponent a free point.
hardHint: A 'Foot' version of this happens if you step on the baseline before making contact.

easyHint: A serve that the opponent fails to even touch.
mediumHint: John Isner holds the all-time record for the most of these in tennis history.
hardHint: Ivo Karlovic was almost unplayable on grass because of his height and ability to hit these constantly.

easyHint: Scoring one over par on a golf hole.
mediumHint: A 'Double' version of this is two over par and usually ruins a scorecard.
hardHint: In the 1890s, this term originally meant the expected score for a good amateur, before 'par' took over.

easyHint: A punch thrown upwards aiming for the opponent's chin.
mediumHint: Mike Tyson's peek-a-boo style relied heavily on this devastating blow.
hardHint: The 'Bolo' punch is a flashy, looping variation of this, popularized by Sugar Ray Leonard.

easyHint: Bringing an opponent to the ground from a standing position.
mediumHint: Freestyle wrestling awards 2 points for executing this move.
hardHint: The 'Double Leg' is the most common, but the 'Suplex' is the most spectacular.

easyHint: A batter accumulates three strikes and is out.
mediumHint: Nolan Ryan holds the all-time MLB record for doing this to batters.
hardHint: If the catcher drops the third strike, the batter can actually attempt to run to first base.

easyHint: A serve that hits the net cord but still lands in the correct box.
mediumHint: When this happens, the serve is simply re-taken with no penalty.
hardHint: In the fast-paced Next Gen ATP Finals, they experimented with abolishing this rule entirely.

easyHint: Sinking the ball directly from the tee box.
mediumHint: Traditionally, the person who achieves this has to buy drinks for everyone at the clubhouse.
hardHint: The odds of a standard amateur golfer hitting this are roughly 12,500 to 1.

easyHint: The super-metal found primarily in Wakanda.
mediumHint: Captain America's iconic shield is composed almost entirely of it.
hardHint: It completely absorbs kinetic energy, but was once broken by Thanos' double-bladed sword.

easyHint: The indestructible metal coating Wolverine's skeleton.
mediumHint: Weapon X bonded this forcefully to Logan's bones.
hardHint: In the comics, an alloy of this and Vibranium is what actually makes up Cap's shield.

easyHint: The magical cosmic metal forged in the heart of a dying star.
mediumHint: The dwarves of Nidavellir use this to craft weapons for the Asgardians.
hardHint: Stormbreaker and Mjolnir are both made from it, requiring extreme heat to mold.

easyHint: Six colored gems of immense power that control aspects of the universe.
mediumHint: Thanos collected them in a gauntlet to wipe out half of all life.
hardHint: In the TVA, these are useless paperweights stored in a desk drawer.

easyHint: A glowing blue cube that houses the Space Stone.
mediumHint: Red Skull used it to power Hydra weapons in WWII.
hardHint: A cat named Goose swallowed this whole in the 1990s.

easyHint: The dark, fluid-like substance that is actually the Reality Stone.
mediumHint: Jane Foster accidentally absorbed it in Thor: The Dark World.
hardHint: Malekith the Dark Elf intended to use it to plunge the Nine Realms into eternal darkness.

easyHint: The metallic casing that contained the Power Stone.
mediumHint: Star-Lord danced his way to stealing it on the planet Morag.
hardHint: Ronan the Accuser hammered it into his Cosmi-Rod to betray Thanos.

easyHint: The magical amulet worn by Doctor Strange.
mediumHint: It originally housed the green Time Stone.
hardHint: Thanos crushed a fake version of this in Strange's hand on Titan.

easyHint: Whosoever holds this hammer, if they be worthy, shall possess the power of Thor.
mediumHint: Captain America famously lifted it in Avengers: Endgame.
hardHint: Hela shattered this effortlessly with one hand in Norway.

easyHint: A massive axe forged to kill Thanos.
mediumHint: Groot chopped off his own arm to provide the handle for it.
hardHint: It has the innate ability to summon the Bifrost.

easyHint: The glowing circle in Tony Stark's chest.
mediumHint: It originally kept shrapnel from entering his heart.
hardHint: Pepper Potts framed the first one with the inscription 'Proof That Tony Stark Has A Heart'.

easyHint: The science juice that allows Ant-Man to shrink or grow.
mediumHint: Without these, the Avengers' 'Time Heist' would have been impossible.
hardHint: Darren Cross tried to replicate this formula and called his version the Yellowjacket.

easyHint: The wrist devices Peter Parker uses to swing around New York.
mediumHint: Tobey Maguire's Spider-Man didn't need these, as his powers were organic.
hardHint: Tony Stark upgraded these to include 576 possible combinations in Homecoming.

easyHint: A small two-finger brass knuckle used to open magical portals.
mediumHint: Doctor Strange left Ned one of these, which he used to summon two other Spider-Men.
hardHint: Kaecilius and his zealots stole the ritual to use these without the Ancient One's permission.

easyHint: The Book of the Damned used by the Scarlet Witch.
mediumHint: It corrupts the mind of anyone who reads it, leading to multiversal chaos.
hardHint: Originally introduced in Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D, but fully weaponized in Multiverse of Madness.

easyHint: The weapon used by Gorr the God Butcher.
mediumHint: It corrupts its user and slowly drains their life force.
hardHint: In the comics, it is bonded to Knull, the creator of the Symbiotes like Venom.

easyHint: Ancient mystical armbands used by Shang-Chi's father.
mediumHint: Wenwu used them to build a criminal empire over a thousand years.
hardHint: They were emitting a mysterious beacon into deep space at the end of Shang-Chi's origin movie.

easyHint: A microscopic universe where time and space behave entirely differently.
mediumHint: Scott Lang was trapped here for five years, which felt like five hours to him.
hardHint: Janet van Dyne survived here for decades, secretly dealing with Kang the Conqueror.

easyHint: The infinite number of parallel realities and alternate timelines.
mediumHint: America Chavez has the unique power to punch star-shaped portals through it.
hardHint: An 'Incursion' happens when two universes collide and destroy each other here.

easyHint: The bureaucratic organization that monitors timelines.
mediumHint: They prune branches to prevent multiversal wars.
hardHint: Mobius loves jet skis, but works for this entity created by He Who Remains.

easyHint: The singular flow of time dictated by the Time Keepers.
mediumHint: Loki stepping out of line in 2012 disrupted this delicate flow.
hardHint: It physically resembles a massive glowing ring wrapping around a citadel at the end of time.

easyHint: A choice or action that creates a branch in the timeline.
mediumHint: Sylvie and Loki touching hands on Lamentis-1 created a massive one of these.
hardHint: In WandaVision, a fake commercial advertises an anti-depressant named after this phenomenon.

easyHint: An alternate version of a character from a different timeline.
mediumHint: There is a version of Loki that is literally just an alligator.
hardHint: Deadpool encounters a swarm of these, including a dog and a floating head, in the void.

easyHint: The gas that triggers the mutation of Inhumans.
mediumHint: Ms. Marvel (Kamala Khan) originally gained her powers in the comics from this.
hardHint: In the MCU TV shows, fish oil pills contaminated with this triggered a wave of new powered people.

easyHint: A bio-hack formula that allows users to regenerate limbs and breathe fire.
mediumHint: Aldrich Killian used this to create exploding super-soldiers in Iron Man 3.
hardHint: Maya Hansen developed this in 1999, but Tony Stark solved its explosive flaw while drunk on a roof.
